name: Baal Extended
description: Baal, meaning 'Lord' or 'Master,' was a significant deity in the Canaanite pantheon, often associated with fertility, storms, and kingship. Baal's origins trace back to various regional manifestations, eventually coalescing into a prominent figure worshipped throughout the Levant. He was believed to control rain, thunder, and lightning, crucial elements for agricultural prosperity in the arid region. Baal's primary role was as a storm god, bringing life-giving rain to nourish the land and ensure bountiful harvests. He was also considered a warrior god, defending his people against enemies and chaos. Baal's power extended to fertility, ensuring the continuation of life, both human and agricultural. His worship often involved elaborate rituals and offerings, aimed at appeasing him and securing his favor. The most prominent myth surrounding Baal is his conflict with Mot, the god of death and the underworld. This epic struggle symbolized the cyclical nature of life, death, and resurrection, mirroring the seasons and the agricultural cycle. Baal's triumph over Mot brought renewed life and prosperity to the land, while his defeat plunged the world into drought and famine. His sister-consort Anat played a vital role in his resurrection. He was a rival to Yam, the god of the sea, whom he defeated with weapons crafted by Kothar-wa-Khasis, the divine craftsman. Baal's association with kingship further solidified his importance in Canaanite society, as rulers often sought to legitimize their power by aligning themselves with the god of fertility and strength.
subtitle: Lord of the Storm and Fertility

keyMyths.title: Baal vs. Yam
keyMyths.description: Baal battles Yam, the god of the sea, for supremacy. Baal triumphs with weapons crafted by Kothar-wa-Khasis, establishing his dominance.

keyMyths.title: Baal vs. Mot
keyMyths.description: Baal confronts Mot, the god of death, leading to his temporary demise. Anat, Baal's sister, intervenes to restore him to life.

keyMyths.title: Construction of Baal's Temple
keyMyths.description: Baal requests a temple be built for him, signifying his elevated status. The construction marks his formal recognition as a supreme deity.

sources.author: Mark S. Smith
sources.work: The Ugaritic Baal Cycle, Volume I
sources.description: Analysis and translation of the Baal Cycle texts.
sources.text: Analysis and translation of the Baal Cycle texts.
